prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
I now have a complete picture. Here is the full diagnosis.

---

## Diagnosis

The live site still shows old content because **editing `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` does not update the live content.** It never could. The file save was successful, but it changed the wrong thing entirely.

---

## Root Cause

### The two-layer architecture you must never confuse

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 1 — Seeding Route (TypeScript source)            │
│  src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts            │
│                                                         │
│  Role: a one-shot seed script that runs ONCE to         │
│  push initial content INTO Payload CMS.                 │
│  It is not the content. It is a trigger.                │
│  After it runs, it has zero effect on the live site.    │
└───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                        │ called via HTTP GET (one time)
                        ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 2 — Payload CMS Database (PostgreSQL / SQLite)   │
│                                                         │
│  Role: the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for all content.      │
│  The live site reads ONLY from here at runtime.         │
│  Editing the route.ts file above changes nothing here.  │
└───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                        │ queried at request time
                        ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 3 — Rendered Page                               │
│  src/app/(frontend)/work/etro/page.tsx (or similar)     │
│                                                         │
│  Reads from Payload. Displays what the DB contains.     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

When you rewrote the content inside `route.ts`, you changed a TypeScript string literal. The Payload database — which the live site queries — was never touched. The route only writes to the database **when it is explicitly called via HTTP**. Saving the file does not invoke it.

This is identical to **ENG-204** (the prior incident), where edits to `update-elan/route.ts` were overwriting CMS deletions on every call — the inverse of this bug, but the same root confusion: **the seeding route is not the content store; it is a one-time write trigger.**

The applicable hard guardrail is **guardrail-engineering-12**: *"Always trace data flow (source → build → server → browser) when debugging visibility issues."* The trace here would have immediately revealed that the render path reads from Payload, not from the route file.

---

## Fix

You have two options depending on the situation:

### Option A — Re-invoke the seeding route (correct for large rewrites)

After your edits to `route.ts` are saved, **call the route** so it actually executes the `payload.update(...)` or `payload.create(...)` calls inside it:

```bash
# Dev environment — confirm port first per docs/port-registry.md
curl -s http://localhost:4001/api/update-etro | jq .

# Then verify what landed in the DB:
curl -s http://localhost:4001/api/etro | jq '.blurb, .scopeStatement'
```

Check the response for `{ success: true }` or the equivalent. Then reload the page and confirm the new content appears.

> ⚠️ **Before calling the route**, audit it for idempotency. If `route.ts` uses `payload.create()` unconditionally, it will insert a duplicate document. It should use an upsert pattern:
> ```ts
> // Safe pattern — find-or-upsert
> const existing = await payload.find({ collection: 'essays', where: { slug: { equals: 'etro' } } })
> if (existing.docs.length > 0) {
>   await payload.update({ collection: 'essays', id: existing.docs[0].id, data: { ... } })
> } else {
>   await payload.create({ collection: 'essays', data: { ... } })
> }
> ```

### Option B — Edit through the Payload Admin UI (correct for ongoing edits)

For content iteration after the initial seed, **bypass the seeding route entirely** and write directly to the CMS:

```
http://localhost:4001/admin
→ Essays (or the relevant collection)
→ ETRO document
→ Edit blurb, scope statement, section bodies inline
→ Save
```

This is the correct workflow for all post-initial-seed content changes. The seeding route's job is done after first invocation.

---

## Correct Workflow Going Forward

| Situation | Correct action |
|-----------|---------------|
| **First-time setup / fresh DB** | Edit `route.ts`, invoke it via `curl`, verify response |
| **Ongoing content iteration** | Edit in Payload Admin UI at `/admin` — never touch `route.ts` |
| **Rewriting content in a session** | Open `/admin`, navigate to the document, edit fields directly |
| **Bulk content migration / new field** | Update `route.ts` to include the new field, invoke once, then return to Admin UI for iteration |
| **"Site shows old content" debugging** | Trace: does `GET /api/etro` return the new text? If not, the DB was never written. If yes, check ISR/cache. |

---

## Principle

**The seeding route is a one-shot write trigger, not a content store.** Editing it changes a TypeScript string that has no effect until the route is explicitly invoked over HTTP. After the initial seed, the Payload Admin UI is the single source of truth and the only correct place to make content changes. Treat `update-*/route.ts` files the same way you treat a database migration script: they run once, on demand, and are not the data.

This is the same root cause as **ENG-204** (update-elan seeding route). If this pattern recurs a third time, the fix is to promote it to a Hard Guardrail in `AGENTS.md` per §8.2 of `docs/engineering.md`.
